TextENGNew: With no school to attend, I had completely lost track of the days. Only the television's programming schedule kept any semblance of routine alive.

Coverage of what the media was calling the "Hinamizawa Virus Outbreak" dominated the news only during the first days of the quarantine. By the tenth day, it barely rated a mention unless something new had developed.

Out in the wider world, apparently some minister hadn't cut short his golf game when the Hinamizawa crisis began, which led to bickering about procedural lapses, which somehow snowballed into questions about his secretary's salary. ...The news cycle had rolled cheerfully off into utter irrelevance.
As long as the media wasn't covering it, society probably assumed the whole virus crisis had been resolved.
It brought home just how coldly the outside world gorged itself on other people's misfortune.
By this point, most villagers had gotten over their hesitation to step outside, at least for essentials like collecting supplies.

Mion had lately started joking that maybe it wasn't the village that was quarantined but Okinomiya—since we didn't have to go into town to shop, and water and food were being delivered free of charge.
changeReason: Old misinterprets the joke as both the village and Okinomiya being quarantined; the JPN joke is that it's Okinomiya that's actually quarantined (locked out), not the village, since the village gets free deliveries.

TextENGNew: I'd manage a thin smile at that, but the suffocating claustrophobia of ten days under quarantine wasn't something a joke could wash away.
The same was true for Mion herself. Her words sounded breezy and carefree, but the anxiety never quite left her expression.

Moping at home wasn't going to help anything. At least talking to people kept your mind off it. I started volunteering to carry the plastic drinking-water tanks, and looked forward to running into friends at the water distribution point.
Not that it was fun, of course.
When we met, all we did was share our worries with each other. ...Even so. It was still far better than huddling alone under the covers......
"...Kei-chan! You're looking well. No strange spots?"
"Mii-chan, that really isn't funny..."
"Mion! And Rena too! Thank goodness. The phones still aren't working, as always—seeing you both in person is such a relief..."
"Yeah. Lately I've been wishing they'd reopen the school. ...Hau."
...If school reopened, we could all try to remember what ordinary life felt like, and put on as brave a face as we could.
...But no, that won't work. Chie-sensei lives in Okinomiya—she can't get here with the quarantine in place. Our class can't restart...
"Ha—well, it's not every day you get a school closure for an unprecedented catastrophe. Might as well enjoy this lifestyle while it lasts!"
In moments like this, Mion's easy banter was genuinely precious. She could pull the conversation somewhere light—something that felt almost like ordinary life.
...But in the end, it always circled back to our anxieties about the situation.
"Oh, that's right...... Rena, Kei-chan. ...Don't get upset, but listen. This stays between us, all right?"
Mion drew us both into the shadows and lowered her voice.
"...What is it, Mii-chan? What's wrong?"
"Have you learned something new?"
The SDF had given a reasonably thorough explanation at the start of the quarantine, but lately there had been no official announcements about how things stood.
That left us with nothing but word of mouth. But the separate quarantine zones within the village kept interaction between residents limited—even rumors couldn't travel very far.
Fortunately, the village had a fair number of amateur radio enthusiasts. They were using their sets to stay in contact with one another.
Mion was one of them—using her radio to check in with the other quarantined zones, trying to piece together a picture of Hinamizawa as a whole.
"Well, the phones still haven't come back. Radios are absolutely critical right now. ...So what have you found out?"
".....................Yes. Well............... It looks like the rumor that Rika-chan was taken into custody and her condition is unknown—it's true."
Let me back up a little here.
changeReason: Old translates as 'She took a brief pause' which is incorrect; JPN says ここで話は少しだけ遡る (the story goes back a little here), a narrator's aside about a flashback, correctly rendered in the new.

TextENGNew: The Sonozaki family had been urgently requesting that Rika Furude be allowed to make rounds through the village—to ease the fears of those who suspected Oyashiro-sama's curse. But those requests had gone unanswered.

The SDF was keeping each council zone strictly isolated to prevent any potential carrier from moving through the village and spreading infection. This meant no one could get word of Rika Furude, which only deepened the villagers' unease.
If something had happened to her—the living reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama—then that could only mean one thing. The end the old legends spoke of. The village's destruction.

That fear had run its course to its natural conclusion: the lack of news about Rika Furude must mean something had happened to her, and the SDF was deliberately concealing it—that rumor had been born and was spreading.
Everyone denied it, of course. But the only way to put the rumor to rest was for the villagers to see Rika Furude alive and well. That wasn't happening, and the collective anxiety was only growing...
"What do you mean, no one knows how Rika-chan is doing?! Are you saying Rika-chan was struck down by the virus?!"

"Shh!! Keep your voice down! ...I don't know if it was the virus, but she was taken in by the epidemic control unit and hasn't been seen since. No one knows if she's safe. Multiple people witnessed her being taken away—that part is certain."
"When did that happen?"
"The day the quarantine began."
"...And what about Satoko-chan?! She lives with Rika-chan, so... well..."
If Rika-chan had been taken as a victim of the virus, then Satoko, living with her, would have been...
"I don't have any information on that..."
"...Of course not. The village elders care deeply about Rika-chan, but Satoko is another matter entirely—they've never wanted anything to do with her."
"Do you think Satoko-chan was taken into custody too......?"
"...It doesn't sound like Satoko was taken in. ...Though basic epidemic protocol would be to suspect anyone living with an infected person."
"...From what I'm hearing on the radio, only Rika-chan was taken. Satoko wasn't, apparently. ......And there's something else... this part gets a little strange..."
"Strange...?"
"...Yeah. .........So, you know how Satoko's parents were supporters of the dam construction project, right?"
"And how afterward, they died in an accident—Oyashiro-sama's curse come down on them..."
"...You mean the rumor that Satoko-chan and the Houjou family are cursed—that Oyashiro-sama despises them?"

"Right. ...There's always been a vicious group of people saying that for someone cursed like Satoko to live alongside the sacred reincarnation—Rika-chan—might taint her. That's been going around for a long time..."
"And now those people are starting to say that this whole thing—this 'curse of Oyashiro-sama'—is Satoko's fault."
"What?! That's absolutely insane!! Those people would blame Satoko if a pair of chopsticks fell over!!"
changeReason: New preserves the specific JPN idiom 箸が転んでも (even if chopsticks fell over) about blaming Satoko, while old uses the generic English idiom 'at the drop of a hat,' losing the cultural flavor.

TextENGNew: "I know it's nonsense, I do. ...But there's apparently a rumor circulating in one of the other council zones—being spoken as if it's perfectly reasonable—that wrapping Satoko in a straw mat and sinking her in Onigafuchi Swamp would appease Oyashiro-sama's wrath..."
changeReason: New correctly identifies the specific location as 'Onigafuchi Swamp' (鬼ヶ淵) and method as 'wrapping in a straw mat' (簀巻き), details the old renders generically as 'binding up' and 'the swamp.'

TextENGNew: "That's—! Are we savages now?! What happened to civilization?! It hasn't even been a week since our daily lives were disrupted, and people are already talking about drowning someone in a swamp?!"
Rena's outrage was completely justified. ...But thinking back on it, Hinamizawa had been in the grip of these superstitions long before the virus commotion began.
This village had never truly proven itself civilized. It only wore the mask. Scratch the surface, and ignorance and blind superstition were always right there underneath...
"...Judging by your face, Mion... this isn't going to end with 'just kidding,' is it..."
Mion's downcast silence was her answer.
"O-Of course I've been telling them over the radio to calm down! But it's not getting through to them at all..."
"God... If Rika-chan's been taken into custody, that means Satoko is living in that shed alone. ...If someone acts on this—isn't she in real danger?!"
"That's right. There's only so much Satoko-chan can do by herself. ...Wouldn't it be better to shelter her somewhere? Wouldn't it?"
Rena's suggestion was sound. If we left Satoko alone, whoever had fallen prey to these delusions might come for her at any moment.
But whose home would she stay in?
According to Mion, rumors that Satoko was already infected were being whispered as though they were gospel truth.

Word of mouth—that most primitive of communication methods—was all Hinamizawa had. And it was terrifyingly easy for rumors to slip into such a network. Mass psychology would latch onto a claim, embroider it, and elevate it to established fact.
Once that happened, it wouldn't matter what the truth was. Satoko would effectively become an infected person. And whatever household took her in would be treated the same way...
Needless to say, Mion's family—the Sonozaki family—had been at odds with Satoko's family, the Houjous, from the beginning. ...It would be extremely difficult for Mion to shelter her.
What about my house? ...Mom would probably understand. But Dad, still fanatically refusing any contact with the outside world, was another matter entirely. I couldn't imagine him agreeing.
"...Yeah. I know it would be impossible for your families. So if we do end up sheltering Satoko-chan, she'll stay at my house. ...Don't worry. I won't let my father object."
I didn't fully understand why, but Rena had control of her family's finances—and when she was serious, even her father couldn't hold out against her.

It stung, not being able to stand up and say my family could take her too. And it stung more that she'd already written us off. But there was nothing to be done about it. ...And petty pride had no place here.

"Understood. If we can reach Satoko, we'll tell her to go to Rena's house. In fact, I'd like to find some way to get in contact with her and bring her here ourselves. ...From what you've told us, it sounds like the area around Satoko's house is getting dangerous..."
changeReason: Old says 'I wish I could reach out to her myself, but...' implying it's impossible; new correctly captures the proactive intent of 'I'd like to find some way to contact her and bring her here ourselves' (何とか沙都子と接触をして、こちらへ呼びたい).

TextENGNew: "There are a lot of zealous members of the shrine committee living around there...... But honestly, Satoko isn't the only one you need to worry about. .........Depending on how things develop... both of you might be in danger too."
Mion's eyes sharpened further as they moved between Rena and me.
That cold look cut right through the naive sense—despite worrying about Satoko—that we ourselves would somehow remain untouched.

"I mentioned this the other day. The rumor that this virus might be native to Hinamizawa. It's gaining real traction now. Some people are even saying every villager has been carrying it for centuries."
"That makes no sense...! If that were true, people would have been dying by the dozens every single day since ancient times!"
"Well... that's where the argument the believers are making gets interesting... They're saying the virus stays stable and harmless as long as you remain in the village."
"They go even further—apparently families who have lived in this village for a very long time are born with a kind of natural immunity that protects them."
"...I don't follow. So what? ...Are you telling me they're about to say that people like Rena and me—who moved here from outside—are a risk because we lack that immunity?"

"That's exactly it! They're saying that people who've moved into Hinamizawa in recent years are disrupting the balance with the virus... and that's making the virus go wild in everyone... and, uh... hmm..."
"...You're not telling us you actually believe this absurd theory, are you, Mii-chan...?"
"Of course not!! There's absolutely no way I'd fall for something that ridiculous!! But... for some reason, everyone else is believing it!! I genuinely don't understand!!"
"Normally no one would ever take such idiotic nonsense seriously! But these past few days, all this strange talk and misinformation is just... circulating like it's common sense!"

"I've been listening to the radio since the very first day. And lately, the villagers are becoming more and more... wrong. I don't know if it's stress from living in abnormal conditions, or trauma, but that framing is too gentle now!"
changeReason: Old says villagers are 'believing weirder stuff' (about beliefs); new correctly captures おかしくなってる as the villagers themselves 'becoming more and more wrong/abnormal,' a broader deterioration than just beliefs.

TextENGNew: "Outright lies and rumors are spreading unchecked, and no one is stopping them! Anyone who tries to contradict them gets labeled a carrier!"
"It's becoming really frightening out there! Something is wrong with people!! So please, be careful!"
The desperate urgency on Mion's face left both Rena and me speechless.
We were clearly fortunate not to have heard what she'd been intercepting on the radio.

Thrust into these abnormal conditions, frustration had reached its peak—and fear and anxiety rooted in old faith were generating new rumors one after another. The situation was approaching the point where only offering up a sacrifice could release the pressure...
From the look on Mion's face, we were already close to that critical threshold.
...And then, for the first time, I became aware of it.
The open hostility in the looks some of the villagers gave us as we talked.
It wasn't my imagination. Rena had caught it too.
".....................Keiichi-kun. ...Let's be careful."
"Y-...Yeah. I don't like being suspected because of stupid rumors, but... you know what they say about standing too close to the plum tree..."
changeReason: New preserves the JPN proverb 李下に冠 (don't adjust your crown under a plum tree = avoid appearing suspicious), while old replaces it with generic 'best not to fuel the fire,' losing the cultural reference.

TextENGNew: "...Yeah. Rena, Kei-chan—both of you should avoid going out unless you have to. And never go out alone. Keep your shutters closed."
"...And if anyone says something thoughtless to you—no matter how infuriating—don't give them anything back. ...All right...?"
The occasional hostile looks I felt now were too real to explain away.
Centuries of isolation had embedded certain accursed customs into this village—and they weren't going to be scrubbed away by a scant few years of modernity...
Rena and I could only nod at Mion's warning, our faces tight...
